# Operational Challenges in the Chemical Sector: How IoT Help

by Ryan Williamson

In the chemicals sector, operational excellence has always been a critical necessity, which is understandable, especially when you consider how this industry serves the world around us. The sector has long been at the forefront of technological advancements, constantly pushing the boundaries of innovation. Unfortunately, despite this sector's super important role and output in the global economy, the chemicals sector is subject to myriad operational challenges. What challenges, you ask.  
  
Well, there are plenty: from complex supply chain management issues to stringent regulatory compliance challenges — there is plenty to contend with. However, significant challenges come with great strides, and the chemical industry is not immune to the operational hurdles that impede efficiency and safety. In recent years, the Internet of Things (IoT) has emerged as a game-changer, offering a comprehensive solution to address the operational challenges faced by the chemical sector.  
  
In this blog post, I will explore how IoT revolutionizes the industry and paves the way for a safer, more efficient future.  
  
Chemical Sector and Its Operational Challenges:  
  
The chemical industry is brimming with intricate processes and high-risk operations. This automatically translates into grappling with various issues, such as process optimization, safety compliance, and asset management, among other things, daily. So, how do companies involved in the chemical business deal with these operational challenges? Honestly, such issues need innovative solutions that can optimize processes and help companies enhance their overall efficiency, productivity, and sustainability levels. While this task is easier said than done, the fact remains that IoT offers potential here.  
  
If you are still unsure about how IoT can help the chemical sector deal with its challenges, read on to learn the many ways this technology can help this industry.  
  
IoT in the Chemicals Sector:  
  
● Hazard prevention: Safety is critically important in the chemicals industry, even though it may seem tricky to ensure here. Thankfully, not when you have IoT in your arsenal because sensors can be leveraged to keep a close eye on vital and relevant parameters, including but not limited to pressure, temperature, vibration, and gas levels, across chemical plants and storage facilities. This real-time data, in turn, can and must be analyzed to ensure that any potential hazards can be identified and, thus, alerts can be sent out before any incident occurs.  
  
● Quality monitoring: Yet another non-negotiable regarding the chemicals industry is quality. But wait, how can IoT help in this regard? In plenty of ways, for starters, companies can integrate IoT devices and sensors across their production lines and in equipment to closely track pre-decided product quality attributes such as product composition, viscosity, and other relevant attributes.  
  
● Plant asset management: Because the chemicals industry depends crucially on expensive assets, including distillation columns, storage tanks, and reactors, it is rendered vital that companies operating such assets also always ensure their proper management. Here, IoT can help by providing data on the real-time performance and state of the identified plant assets. Once this gathered data is analyzed, companies can gain powerful insights to help them foresee potential equipment failures and proactively schedule maintenance.  
  
Final Words  
It is clear as day sees that IoT has firmly established itself as a catalyst for better transforming the chemical sector. The integration of IoT in the chemical industry marks a significant leap forward in addressing operational challenges. From improving safety and compliance to optimizing processes and reducing downtime, IoT technologies offer a holistic solution that aligns with the industry's innovation, sustainability, and efficiency goals. As the chemical sector continues to embrace the potential of IoT, we can anticipate a future where intelligent, interconnected systems redefine the landscape of chemical manufacturing. From ensuring top-notch levels of security across operations to ensuring sustainable growth, IoT can help with plenty. Go ahead, then — start looking for a trusted company for developing IoT apps for your business, too, won't you? Get at https://www.rishabhsoft.com/services/iot-development

# Chemical Industry's Margin Erosion: A Wake-Up Call for Operational Efficiency Amid China's Oversupply

![Chemical Industry's Margin Erosion: A Wake-Up Call for Operational Efficiency Amid China's Oversupply](https://www.innovapptive.com/hubfs/images/SR%20BG%202_1.jpg)

The global chemical industry is facing mounting challenges, with revenue declines and shrinking profit margins becoming increasingly common. One key driver of this downturn is China's aggressive expansion in chemical production, resulting in an oversupply of low-cost alternatives. This situation serves as a critical wake-up call for chemical industry executives to prioritize operational efficiency and initiatives aimed at improving EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization) to remain competitive.

## The Impact of China's Oversupply and Low-Cost Production

China has become a dominant force in the global chemical industry. In 2022, [China accounted for 44% of global chemical production and 46% of global capital investment](https://itif.org/publications/2024/04/15/how-innovative-is-china-in-the-chemicals-industry/). This rapid growth has been driven by massive investments and a labor pool willing to work for lower wages, enabling Chinese chemical companies to produce and export chemicals at significantly lower costs.

The influx of low-cost chemical products from China has triggered a global oversupply, leading to a [decrease in prices and putting pressure on profit margins for chemical companies worldwide](https://cen.acs.org/business/Chinas-chemical-industry-hopes-for-a-turnaround/103/i1). This oversupply has been particularly harmful to companies in high-cost regions such as Europe and North America, where labor and energy costs are significantly higher.

## The Need for Operational Efficiency and EBITDA Improvement

In this volatile environment, chemical manufacturers must shift their focus from traditional growth strategies to enhancing operational efficiency and improving EBITDA. Many chemical companies still operate with outdated work management systems and suboptimal tools, resulting in inefficiencies that drain resources.

Research indicates that up to one-third of maintenance costs in the chemical industry are unnecessary or poorly executed. Tackling these inefficiencies offers significant opportunities for cost savings and margin improvement. Focusing on maintenance cost reduction, workforce efficiency, and operational process optimization can have an immediate positive impact on profitability.  
  
![SR BG 2_2](https://www.innovapptive.com/hs-fs/hubfs/images/SR%20BG%202_2.jpg?width=560&height=336&name=SR%20BG%202_2.jpg)

![SR BG 2_2](https://www.innovapptive.com/hs-fs/hubfs/images/SR%20BG%202_2.jpg?width=560&height=336&name=SR%20BG%202_2.jpg)

Flashy Digital Projects Prioritization without Accountability of EBITDA Impact under 6 months

## Avoiding the Allure of Flashy Digital Projects

While digital transformation is essential, many chemical companies have been sidetracked by flashy technologies that promise innovation but fail to deliver tangible returns. Investments in technologies like Digital Twins, DataOps Platforms, Augmented Reality (AR/VR), and AI-Powered Connected Worker Platforms often lack clear links to EBITDA improvement.

Consulting firms such as McKinsey and Bain&Company emphasize the importance of aligning digital initiatives with specific operational goals. For example, digital manufacturing transformation projects that focus on work management process improvements have been shown to reduce maintenance costs by 15-30% and increase overall productivity by 10%. Chemical companies should prioritize digital investments that deliver measurable efficiency gains and cost reductions, directly impacting EBITDA.

## Real World Impact: $8M - $12M in Annualized Savings

A case in point is a $24 billion chemical company that implemented [Innovapptive](https://www.innovapptive.com)’s solutions at one of its plants. The results were striking:

These improvements highlight the tangible benefits of targeted digital initiatives in maintenance management and operational excellence, demonstrating significant EBITDA growth.

## Setting Clear Goals for Maintenance Spend Reduction

Up to one-third of maintenance spending in the chemical industry is wasteful. Companies should set ambitious goals to reduce maintenance costs by at least 10%. Achieving this requires a comprehensive approach:

## Conclusion: Achieving Sustainable Growth Through Operational Excellence

The ongoing challenges faced by the chemical industry, compounded by China’s oversupply and low-cost production, demand a strategic shift towards operational efficiency and EBITDA-focused initiatives. By addressing legacy inefficiencies and aligning digital transformation projects with tangible operational goals, chemical companies can enhance their competitiveness, drive cost reductions, and ensure long-term sustainable growth in an increasingly competitive global market.

Ready to transform your chemical manufacturing operations? Start driving cost savings and improving EBITDA today. Explore Innovapptive’sAI-drivensolutions to boost operational efficiency and optimize your maintenance strategies for maximum profitability.

--- SOURCE 5: 2025 Chemical Industry Outlook | Deloitte Insights ---
URL: https://www2.deloitte.com/us/en/insights/industry/oil-and-gas/chemical-industry-outlook.html

SUMMARY:
The chemical industry has made moderate progress in 2024, increasing year-over-year production above 2023 levels, and it is estimated that production levels will continue to rise as the destocking cycle wanes and demand rises across most products.1 However, to further support revenue growth, chemical companies have announced cost-reduction plans and begun to increase margins, while continuing to invest in decarbonization and innovation.2 In 2025, we expect the industry to continue its recovery, adjusting to new market drivers while balancing short- and long-term goals.

### Ashlee Christian

United States

[_aschristian@deloitte.com_](mailto:aschristian@deloitte.com "mail")

![Image 17](https://www2.deloitte.com/content/dam/insights/icons/svg/icon-alert.svg)

The chemical industry has made moderate progress in 2024, increasing year-over-year production above 2023 levels, and it is estimated that production levels will continue to rise as the destocking cycle wanes and demand rises across most products.[1](https://www2.deloitte.com/us/en/insights/industry/oil-and-gas/chemical-industry-outlook.html#endnote-1) However, to further support revenue growth, chemical companies have announced cost-reduction plans and begun to increase margins, while continuing to invest in decarbonization and innovation.[2](https://www2.deloitte.com/us/en/insights/industry/oil-and-gas/chemical-industry-outlook.html#endnote-2) In 2025, we expect the industry to continue its recovery, adjusting to new market drivers while balancing short- and long-term goals.

![Image 18](https://www2.deloitte.com/content/dam/insights/icons/svg/icon-alert.svg)

![Image 19](https://www2.deloitte.com/content/dam/insights/icons/svg/icon-alert.svg)

Since the COVID-19 pandemic began in 2020, the chemical industry has had to navigate turbulent market conditions. The pandemic led to weak demand, reduced production, and lower revenues in 2020. This was followed by a strong rebound in chemical production and revenues from 2021 to 2022, as demand rose, and fear of supply chain issues led to higher levels of inventory stocking. But by late 2022, supply chain issues began to wane, key end markets began destocking, and demand for chemicals declined. By the end of 2023, revenues decreased 8% year on year, operating margins fell to their lowest level since the Great Recession (2007 to 2009), and returns on capital dropped back to pre-pandemic levels.[3](https://www2.deloitte.com/us/en/insights/industry/oil-and-gas/chemical-industry-outlook.html#endnote-3) These dynamics highlighted a need for greater resilience spurring cost reduction programs. While these cost-reduction programs are still rolling out, margins began to rise in the first half of 2024 (figure 1).[4](https://www2.deloitte.com/us/en/insights/industry/oil-and-gas/chemical-industry-outlook.html#endnote-4)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Chemical plants often depend on outdated manual processes for operations, leading to inefficiencies, slow decision-making, and increased error rates, hindering overall productivity.
The industry faces significant hurdles such as complex supply chains and regulatory compliance issues, which contribute to high operational costs and reduced profit margins.
Integrating real-time data is crucial for optimizing processes and enhancing safety, allowing companies to respond swiftly to operational changes and maintain quality control.
